articles.label: Government
articles.headline: Escambia County's $47 Million Question: Where the Tourist Tax Actually Goes
articles.dek: Bed tax collections hit record highs last fiscal year. A Flightline review of spending records shows the money flows to familiar names—and raises questions about oversight.
brief: Escambia County collected $47.2 million in tourist development taxes in fiscal year 2025, a 12% increase over the previous year
More than 60% of discretionary spending went to five organizations, with Visit Pensacola receiving $18.4 million
County commissioners approved every Tourist Development Council funding recommendation without modification for the past three years
articles.body:
The check arrives every month like clockwork. Visit Pensacola, the county's destination marketing organization, deposits another seven-figure transfer from Escambia County's tourist development fund. In March alone, the organization received $1.53 million.

This is how $47.2 million moves through a Florida county each year—collected from visitors sleeping in hotel rooms and vacation rentals, then distributed by an appointed council that operates largely outside public attention.

A Flightline review of three years of Tourist Development Council records, county commission meeting minutes and state financial disclosures reveals a funding system that functions on autopilot. The same organizations receive the same large allocations year after year. The oversight body charged with guiding these decisions includes members with direct financial ties to the tourism industry. And the elected commissioners who hold final approval have not modified a single TDC recommendation since at least 2023.

Following the Money

Florida law restricts how counties can spend tourist development taxes—the so-called bed tax collected as a percentage of short-term lodging charges. The money must promote tourism or fund tourism-related infrastructure. In Escambia County, that tax sits at 5% of room rates.

The largest slice goes to Visit Pensacola. The organization received $18.4 million in fiscal year 2025 for marketing, advertising and destination promotion. That represents 39% of total collections.

Pensacola Sports received $4.1 million to recruit and host athletic events. The Pensacola Bay Center—the county-owned arena—received $3.8 million for operations and debt service. The Blue Wahoos stadium project continues to draw annual payments. The Gulf Islands National Seashore Foundation received $890,000 for beach maintenance coordination.

These five recipients accounted for more than 60% of all discretionary spending.

The pattern holds across multiple years. In fiscal 2024, Visit Pensacola received $16.9 million. Pensacola Sports received $3.7 million. The same names appear in the same positions on the funding priority list.

The Council in the Room

The Tourist Development Council is not a government body in the traditional sense. It's an advisory panel created under Florida Statute 125.0104, required by law to include representatives from the tourism industry itself.

The current nine-member Escambia County TDC includes three hotel operators, the president of the Perdido Key Association, a vacation rental company executive and the Visit Pensacola CEO. Two county commissioners also serve, along with one at-large appointment.

This composition is not unusual—the statute requires lodging industry representation. But critics argue the structure creates inherent conflicts.

"You have people voting on how to spend money that directly benefits their own businesses," said Janet Kirkland, a Gulf Breeze resident who has attended TDC meetings for the past two years. "The hotels want more marketing. The marketing brings more visitors. The visitors fill the hotels. It's a closed loop."

TDC members are required to file financial disclosure statements with the state. A review of 2025 filings shows four members reported ownership interests in businesses that receive direct or indirect benefits from TDC-funded marketing campaigns.

Florida law does not prohibit these members from voting on funding allocations. The statute's drafters assumed industry expertise would benefit decision-making.

The Rubber Stamp Question

The TDC meets monthly to review funding requests, hear presentations from grant recipients and make recommendations to the county commission. The commission holds final authority over all expenditures.

Meeting minutes from January 2023 through April 2026 show the commission approved every TDC recommendation without modification. Not a single line item was questioned publicly. Not a single allocation was reduced or redirected.

Commissioner Jeff Bergosh, who chairs the commission and serves on the TDC, defended the approval record.

"The TDC does extensive vetting," Bergosh said in an email response to questions. "These are people with deep expertise in what works for tourism marketing. When they reach consensus on a recommendation, that carries weight."

He noted that commissioners receive detailed backup materials before votes and can ask questions during meetings.

Meeting recordings tell a different story. The tourism fund typically appears on the consent agenda—a batch of items approved in a single vote without individual discussion. In the 39 commission meetings reviewed, tourist development allocations received substantive floor discussion exactly twice. Both instances involved new grant applicants, not ongoing funding levels.

What Accountability Looks Like

Visit Pensacola submits quarterly reports to the TDC documenting advertising reach, website traffic and earned media value. The organization reported 2.3 billion "media impressions" in fiscal 2025—a number that includes everything from a Super Bowl ad view to a travel blogger's Instagram post.

What the reports do not show: direct correlation between marketing spending and actual visitation. The organization tracks hotel occupancy rates and airport passenger counts as proxies, but these metrics respond to many variables beyond advertising.

When asked how the county measures return on investment for its $18.4 million marketing expenditure, Visit Pensacola CEO Darien Schaefer pointed to a 2024 economic impact study commissioned by the organization itself.

"The study shows tourism generated $3.2 billion in economic impact for Escambia County," Schaefer said. "Our marketing investment is a fraction of that return."

The study was conducted by Tourism Economics, a subsidiary of Oxford Economics. The methodology relies on visitor spending surveys and multiplier effects—standard practice in the industry, but not without critics who question whether such studies inflate benefits.

No independent audit of Visit Pensacola's effectiveness has been conducted by the county.

Small Grants, Big Questions

Beyond the major recipients, the TDC distributes smaller grants through a competitive application process. Festivals, sporting events and cultural organizations apply for funding to support tourism-generating activities.

The process appears straightforward on paper. Applicants submit detailed proposals. TDC staff score them against established criteria. The council votes on recommendations.

In practice, the same events receive funding year after year. The Pensacola Seafood Festival has received TDC grants for at least 15 consecutive years. The Foo Foo Festival—a newer arts and culture event—has received funding since its 2015 inception.

New applicants face steeper odds. Of 34 first-time grant applications in the past three fiscal years, 11 received funding. Of 89 renewal applications, 84 were approved.

"There's definitely an incumbent advantage," said Marcus Webb, who applied unsuccessfully for TDC funding for a proposed fishing tournament in 2024. "The established events have relationships. They know how to write the applications. They know who to talk to."

Webb's application was denied for insufficient documentation of projected room nights—a key metric in TDC evaluations. He said he was never offered guidance on how to strengthen his proposal.

The Oversight Gap

Florida's tourist development tax statute requires annual audits of TDC expenditures. Escambia County satisfies this requirement through its standard annual financial audit, which includes the tourist development fund as one line item among hundreds.

The audit confirms money was spent according to approved budgets. It does not evaluate whether the spending achieved its intended purpose.

"A compliance audit and a performance audit are two different things," said Catherine Morse, a retired forensic accountant who reviewed the county's tourism records at The Flightline's request. "Compliance asks: Did you follow the rules? Performance asks: Did you get results? One of those questions isn't being asked."

Morse noted that several Florida counties—including Hillsborough and Pinellas—have commissioned independent performance audits of their tourism spending in the past decade. Escambia County has not.

Commissioner Lumon May, who does not serve on the TDC, said he would be open to discussing a performance audit.

"If we're spending this much money, we should know if it's working," May said. "I don't think that's an unreasonable question."

What Happens Next

The fiscal 2027 budget process begins next month. Visit Pensacola has already submitted a request for $19.8 million—a 7.6% increase over current funding. Pensacola Sports seeks $4.5 million. The Gulf Islands National Seashore Foundation wants $1.1 million.

The TDC will review these requests at its June meeting. The county commission will likely vote on approvals in September.

If history holds, every recommendation will pass without modification. The money will flow to familiar names. The tourists will keep coming—or not—and the question of whether $47 million bought anything more than the status quo will go unasked for another year.

The next TDC meeting is scheduled for June 12 at 9 a.m. in the Escambia County Central Office Complex. The meetings are open to the public.

articles.label: Neighborhood
articles.headline: The Last Block of Old East Hill
articles.dek: One street off Cervantes holds eight homes built before 1940. Three developers have made offers this spring. The neighbors aren't selling—yet.
brief: Eight pre-1940 homes on East Brainerd Street represent one of the last intact blocks of original East Hill architecture
Three development companies have approached homeowners with purchase offers since February, with one proposing a 12-unit townhouse project
Residents are organizing to pursue local historic designation, though the process offers limited protection against determined developers
articles.body:
The ceiling fan in Martha Guidry's living room has been spinning since 1987. The house it hangs in has been standing since 1923. She bought both for $34,000 the year her husband died, raised two daughters in the three bedrooms, and buried a dog in the backyard under the crepe myrtle her mother-in-law planted.

Now a man from Destin wants to tear it down.

"He came to my door in March with a folder," Guidry said, sitting on her screened porch on East Brainerd Street, a single block that dead-ends into a drainage easement 200 yards south of Cervantes. "Had pictures of what he wanted to build. Twelve townhouses. Showed me the number he'd pay and said I should think about my age."

She is 78. She told him to leave.

Eight Houses, One Block

East Brainerd runs 400 feet from its intersection with 16th Avenue to a chain-link fence marking the edge of a stormwater retention area. On one side sit four wood-frame bungalows built between 1922 and 1938. On the other, four more of the same vintage. All eight still stand. All eight are occupied.

According to Escambia County property records, the homes range from 1,100 to 1,600 square feet. Most have been modified over the decades—vinyl siding over original clapboard, window units punched through bedroom walls, carports added where driveways used to be. But the bones remain: pier-and-beam foundations, 9-foot ceilings, wraparound porches designed for a time before air conditioning.

The rest of East Hill has changed around them. The 2020 census showed the neighborhood's population grew 12% in the previous decade. Single-family teardowns have given way to duplexes and small apartment buildings along the corridors near Cervantes and 12th Avenue. A four-story mixed-use development broke ground on North Baylen Street in January.

East Brainerd, tucked behind a church parking lot and invisible from any major road, remained untouched. Until this spring.

Three Offers in Three Months

Raymond Tate has lived at 1614 East Brainerd since 2003. He bought his 1926 bungalow from the estate of its original owner, a shipyard worker named Harold Finch who died at 94. Tate, a retired electrician, spent two years rewiring the house himself.

In February, he received a letter from Gulf South Development Partners LLC, a company registered in Okaloosa County. The letter offered $285,000 for his property—roughly $60,000 above its appraised value—and invited him to a "confidential discussion" about the block's future.

"I called my neighbor," Tate said. "She got the same letter. Same number. Same everything."

Property records show Gulf South Development Partners was incorporated in November 2025. Its registered agent is a Destin attorney named Craig Woodham. The company has no other property holdings in Escambia County. Woodham did not respond to requests for comment.

A second offer came in March, this one from a Pensacola-based investor named David Horton. Horton, who owns 14 rental properties in the East Hill and East Pensacola Heights areas, offered $240,000 for Guidry's home and $250,000 for the property next door, owned by a couple in their 60s who declined to be interviewed.

Horton confirmed the offers in a phone interview. "I'm not trying to tear anything down," he said. "I buy houses. I rent houses. That's my business."

Asked whether he would resell to a developer, Horton paused. "I don't control what happens after I own something."

The third approach came two weeks ago. This time it was in person—the man from Destin with the folder, who identified himself to multiple residents as representing Gulf South Development Partners. County building department records show no permit applications or site plans filed for the East Brainerd block.

What Protection Exists

Pensacola's historic preservation ordinance allows neighborhoods to apply for local historic designation, which requires City Council approval and subjects properties to design review for exterior modifications. The designation does not prevent demolition outright but adds procedural hurdles, including a 90-day delay and mandatory review by the city's Architectural Review Board.

Donnie Singh, the city's historic preservation planner, said East Brainerd would likely qualify. "You've got eight contributing structures from the same period, intact setbacks, consistent scale," Singh said. "On paper, it meets the criteria."

The process takes six to eight months. It requires signatures from at least 51% of property owners, a historic survey conducted by a qualified consultant, and public hearings before the Architectural Review Board and City Council.

Singh noted that designation has stopped some projects and slowed others. It did not stop the demolition of three Victorian-era homes on North Palafox Street in 2019, when the property owner accepted the 90-day delay and proceeded with teardowns for a parking lot that was never built.

"It's a tool," Singh said. "It's not a wall."

The Neighbors Talk

On a Thursday evening in late April, six of the eight East Brainerd homeowners gathered in Tate's living room. They sat in a circle of mismatched chairs—two folding, one wicker, three dining—and talked for 90 minutes about what to do.

The conversation was not recorded at the group's request, but residents agreed to summarize their positions afterward.

Guidry and Tate want to pursue historic designation. James and Carolyn Weathers, who have lived at 1618 East Brainerd since 1978, support the idea but worry about restrictions on their ability to modify their own home. A younger couple who bought their house in 2021—the only residents under 50—said they were undecided.

Two homeowners did not attend. One, a woman in her 80s who lives alone at the end of the block, told a neighbor she was considering the offers.

"We can't make her stay," Tate said. "But if one domino falls, they'll have a foothold."

Under Pensacola's zoning code, the East Brainerd block is designated R-1, single-family residential, with a minimum lot size of 5,000 square feet. A 12-unit townhouse development would require rezoning or a variance from the Board of Adjustment. City planning staff said no such application has been filed.

What a Block Remembers

The crepe myrtle in Guidry's backyard blooms pink every June. She can see it from her kitchen window while she washes dishes. Her mother-in-law, Odette, planted it in 1952, the year Guidry's husband was born in the back bedroom.

Guidry's daughters live in Mobile now. They call on Sundays. They have told her, gently, that she might be happier in a smaller place, something with fewer stairs and neighbors her own age. She tells them she is fine.

"This house knows me," she said. "I walk through it in the dark and I know where everything is. The creaky board in the hallway. The window that sticks. You can't buy that."

She has not called the man from Destin back. She does not intend to. But she keeps his folder in a drawer in the kitchen, next to the warranty for the ceiling fan that has been spinning since 1987.

"I know what it's worth to him," she said. "I'm just not sure he knows what it's worth to me."

articles.label: Government
articles.headline: Blue Angels Homecoming Week Brings 200,000 Visitors and a Strained City Budget
articles.dek: Pensacola celebrates its most famous squadron, but police overtime, sanitation costs, and traffic management fall entirely on local taxpayers. City officials say it's time to renegotiate the deal with the Navy.
brief: Blue Angels Homecoming Air Show drew an estimated 200,000 visitors to NAS Pensacola over three days last weekend
City of Pensacola spent $1.2 million on police overtime, traffic control, and sanitation—costs not reimbursed by the Navy
City Council members are calling for a formal cost-sharing agreement before the 2027 show season
articles.body:
The F/A-18 Super Hornets screamed over Bayou Grande at 400 miles per hour Saturday afternoon, trailing white smoke against a cloudless sky. On the flight line at NAS Pensacola, 70,000 people craned their necks upward. Children sat on coolers. Veterans in ball caps wiped their eyes during the national anthem.

Blue Angels Homecoming is Pensacola's biggest weekend of the year. It is also, increasingly, its most expensive.

The three-day air show that concluded Sunday drew an estimated 200,000 visitors to the naval air station, according to figures released by base public affairs. Hotels from Perdido Key to Gulf Breeze reported full occupancy. Restaurants along Palafox Street had two-hour waits.

But when the jets landed and the crowds dispersed, Pensacola taxpayers were left holding a bill the Navy doesn't pay.

$1.2 Million and Counting

City budget documents obtained by The Flightline show Pensacola spent $1.2 million supporting the 2026 Homecoming Air Show. The largest expense: $680,000 in police overtime for traffic control, crowd management, and security details outside the base perimeter.

Sanitation services added another $180,000. City crews collected 47 tons of trash from parking areas, roadsides, and downtown streets between Friday morning and Monday afternoon.

Traffic management—including temporary signal timing changes, barricade deployment, and coordination with the Florida Department of Transportation—cost $140,000. The remainder covered emergency medical services staging, administrative overtime, and fuel.

None of it was reimbursed.

"We love the Blue Angels. Everyone in this city does," said Pensacola City Council member Delores Anderson, who represents District 6. "But love doesn't balance a budget. We're subsidizing a federal event with local property taxes."

A Handshake, Not a Contract

The arrangement between Pensacola and the Navy has never been formalized. There is no memorandum of understanding. No cost-sharing agreement. No written document that defines who pays for what.

City Administrator Keith Wilkins confirmed that the relationship operates on "historical precedent and goodwill."

"The air show has been happening here since 1946," Wilkins said. "The city has always provided support. It's just what we do."

That informal arrangement made sense when the show drew 30,000 people and required a handful of traffic cops. The scale has changed. The deal has not.

NAS Pensacola public affairs officer Lt. Commander Rachel Torres declined to comment on budget negotiations but said the Navy "values its partnership with the City of Pensacola and the surrounding community."

Economic Impact, Unevenly Distributed

Proponents of the status quo point to economic impact. A 2024 study commissioned by Visit Pensacola estimated that Blue Angels air shows generate $45 million annually for the regional economy. Hotels, restaurants, and retailers benefit directly.

But those businesses don't pay the city's overtime bills.

Escambia County collects the tourist development tax—the so-called "bed tax"—on hotel stays. That revenue funds Visit Pensacola's marketing budget and helps pay for beach renourishment. It does not flow to the city's general fund.

"The county gets the bed tax. The hotels get the guests. The restaurants get the customers," Anderson said. "The city gets the traffic and the trash."

She has proposed that the city formally request a cost-sharing agreement with the Navy before the November practice shows and the 2027 Homecoming.

What Other Cities Do

Pensacola is not the only city that hosts Blue Angels performances. The squadron flies roughly 60 shows per year at locations across the country.

In some cities, event organizers pay the full cost of local services. The Cleveland National Air Show, for example, is run by a nonprofit that reimburses the city for police and fire support.

In others, the host installation covers costs through its morale, welfare, and recreation budget or through gate fees and vendor contracts.

Pensacola charges no admission to the Homecoming show. NAS Pensacola operates the event with no gate revenue. Food and merchandise vendors pay fees to the Navy's MWR program, not the city.

"We're the only major air show market I know of where the host city just eats the cost," said former City Council member Brian Spencer, who raised the issue during his tenure from 2018 to 2022. "I never could get anyone to take it seriously."

The Political Reality

Challenging the arrangement carries political risk. The Blue Angels are Pensacola's most visible symbol, featured on welcome signs, tourism brochures, and the logo of the Pensacola Ice Flyers hockey team. Criticizing any aspect of the show can look like criticizing the squadron itself.

Mayor D.C. Reeves has not publicly supported Anderson's proposal. In a statement to The Flightline, his office said the mayor "remains committed to supporting the Blue Angels and will work with all stakeholders to ensure the show's continued success."

The statement did not address cost-sharing.

Council member Teniadé Broughton, who represents District 2, said she supports opening a dialogue with the Navy but cautioned against framing it as adversarial.

"We're not trying to stick the Navy with a bill," Broughton said. "We're trying to build a sustainable partnership. That means having an honest conversation about who pays for what."

Next Steps

Anderson said she plans to introduce a resolution at the May 15 council meeting requesting that the city administrator begin formal negotiations with NAS Pensacola leadership.

She acknowledged that any agreement would require approval from Navy Region Southeast, headquartered in Jacksonville, and possibly from Washington.

"I don't expect this to happen overnight," Anderson said. "But if we don't start the conversation, nothing changes. And we'll be back here next year writing the same check."

The Blue Angels return to Pensacola for their November practice shows before the 2027 season. The city, presumably, will be ready with traffic cones and overtime slips.

Whether it will also have a new deal remains to be seen.
